From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Dutch drug law

Not to be confused with [Aboriginals Protection and Restriction of the Sale of Opium Act 1897](/wiki/Aboriginals_Protection_and_Restriction_of_the_Sale_of_Opium_Act_1897 "Aboriginals Protection and Restriction of the Sale of Opium Act 1897").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **needs more [reliable medical references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Identifying_reliable_sources_(medicine) "Wikipedia:Identifying reliable sources (medicine)") for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") or relies too heavily on [primary sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Primary_sources "Wikipedia:Primary sources")**. Please review the contents of the article and [add the appropriate references](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Opium_Law&action=edit) if you can. Unsourced or poorly sourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *Find sources:* ["Opium Law"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Opium+Law%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Opium+Law%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Opium+Law%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Opium+Law%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Opium+Law%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Opium+Law%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(October 2013)* |

The **Opium Law** (*Opiumwet* in [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language")) is the section of the [Dutch law](/wiki/Law_of_the_Netherlands "Law of the Netherlands") which covers nearly all [psychotropic drugs](/wiki/Psychotropic_drug "Psychotropic drug").

## Origin and history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium_Law&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origin and history")]

In 1912,[[1]](#cite_note-1) the First International Opium Conference took place in [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague"), where agreements were made about the trade in [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium"); this initiated the introduction of the Opium Law, which took place 7 years later. In 1919, the first Opium Law (later known as List I of the Opium Law) was introduced, and on 12 May 1928 the second Opium Law (later known as List II of the Opium Law) was introduced. The first Opium Law was created to regulate drugs with a high addiction or abuse factor, or that are physically harmful. As the name indicates the main reason for introduction was to regulate the Opium trade and later to control various other addictive drugs like [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine"), [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin"), [barbiturates](/wiki/Barbiturates "Barbiturates"), [amphetamines](/wiki/Amphetamines "Amphetamines") and several decades later, [benzodiazapines](/wiki/Benzodiazapines "Benzodiazapines"), which were used both medically and recreationally.

Except for the addition of new drugs to List I and II of the Opium Law, the Opium Law remained unchanged until 1976. After the rise of a new youth culture which revolved much around the use of drugs like [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)") and [LSD](/wiki/LSD "LSD"), and with [hashish](/wiki/Hashish "Hashish") being openly used, a change of law was needed by the government, to properly control all drugs, but with a clear definition between drugs with an unacceptable degree of addictiveness or physical harm (known as [hard drugs](/wiki/Hard_drugs "Hard drugs")), and drugs with an acceptable degree of addictiveness or physical harm (known as [soft drugs](/wiki/Drug_harmfulness "Drug harmfulness")). In 1976 these changes officially took effect, and the Opium Law was edited to include the changes in the law. In the same year, a decision was made by the Dutch government to discontinue prosecuting cannabis and hashish offenses, provided the person did not sell hard drugs, did not advertise and carried less than a specified maximum amount of cannabis or hashish.

In 1980, the decision to not prosecute cannabis and hashish dealers, under certain conditions, was publicly announced by the Dutch government. Many people thereby concluded that this decision would also allow the sale in [coffee shops](/wiki/Cannabis_coffee_shop "Cannabis coffee shop"), and coffee shops began selling cannabis and hashish. This led to an enormous rise in the number of coffee shops in the 80's and 90's, and because of this, new regulations were demanded by the government to regulate the sale of cannabis products by coffee shops. In 1996 the laws were changed again to include new regulations for coffee shops. The terms coffee shops had to follow were:

* No advertisement
* No hard drugs
* No entrance to coffee shops by persons under the age of 18
* No sale of more than 5 grams of cannabis products per person, per day
* Coffee shops are not allowed to have more than 500 grams of cannabis in stock at any time

Since 01-01-2020, no new changes have been made to the Opium Law. Most of the changes in law since 1996 have been additions of new psychoactive substances, [ADB-FUBINACA](/wiki/ADB-FUBINACA "ADB-FUBINACA") has been one of the latest on this list being added on 01-01-2020. New guidelines for coffee shops have been made, but they are not covered by the Opium Law.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## List I drugs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium_Law&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: List I drugs")]

The following drugs and intermediates are classified as List I drugs of the Opium Law:

The esters and derivatives of ecgonine, which can be turned into ecgonine and cocaine;

The mono- and [di-alkylamide-](/wiki/LSD "LSD"), the pyrrolidine- and morpholine derivatives of [lysergic acid](/wiki/Lysergic_acid "Lysergic acid"), and the thereby introduction of methyl-, acetyl- or halogen groups obtained substances;

Fiveworthy nitrogen-substituted morphinederivates, of which morphine-N-oxide-derivatives, like codeine-N-oxide;

The isomers and stereoisomers of tetrahydrocannabinol;

The [ethers](/wiki/Ether "Ether"), [esters](/wiki/Ester "Ester") and [enantiomers](/wiki/Enantiomer "Enantiomer") of the above mentioned substances, with exception of [dextromethorphan](/wiki/Dextromethorphan "Dextromethorphan") (INN) as enantiomer of levomethorphan and racemethorphan, and with exception of dextrorphanol (INN) as enantiomere of levorphanol and racemorphan;

Formulations which contain one or more of the above mentioned substances.[[6]](#cite_note-auto-6)

†Formulations of 20 mg or more of temazepam are classed under List I.

## List II drugs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium_Law&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: List II drugs")]

The following drugs are classified as List II drugs of the Opium Law:

* [allobarbital](/wiki/Allobarbital "Allobarbital")
* [alprazolam](/wiki/Alprazolam "Alprazolam")

* [ethylloflazepate](/wiki/Ethylloflazepate "Ethylloflazepate")
* [ethylamphetamine](/wiki/Ethylamphetamine "Ethylamphetamine")
* [fencamfamine](/wiki/Fencamfamine "Fencamfamine")
* [fenproporex](/wiki/Fenproporex "Fenproporex")
* [fludiazepam](/wiki/Fludiazepam "Fludiazepam")
* [flunitrazepam](/wiki/Flunitrazepam "Flunitrazepam")

* [glutethimide](/wiki/Glutethimide "Glutethimide")

* [haloxazolam](/wiki/Haloxazolam "Haloxazolam")
* [hashish](/wiki/Hashish "Hashish") (a usually solid mixture of the excreted resin obtained from plants of the Cannabis species (hemp), with plant materials of these plants)
* [hemp](/wiki/Hemp "Hemp") (all parts of the plant from the Cannabis species (hemp), of which the resin has not been extracted, with exception of the seeds)
* [ketazolam](/wiki/Ketazolam "Ketazolam")
* [lefetamine](/wiki/Lefetamine "Lefetamine")
* [loprazolam](/wiki/Loprazolam "Loprazolam")

* [lormetazepam](/wiki/Lormetazepam "Lormetazepam")

* [mefenorex](/wiki/Mefenorex "Mefenorex")
* [meprobamate](/wiki/Meprobamate "Meprobamate")
* [mesocarb](/wiki/Mesocarb "Mesocarb")

* [oxazepam](/wiki/Oxazepam "Oxazepam")

## Medical use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Opium_Law&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Medical use")]

Even though List I substances are officially classified as hard drugs, several of them are often prescribed by licensed [doctors](/wiki/Physician "Physician"). For example, nearly all [opioids](/wiki/Opioids "Opioids") are List I drugs, but they are commonly prescribed to cancer and HIV patients, as well as sufferers of chronic pain. Two [stimulants](/wiki/Stimulants "Stimulants") which are both prescribed for [ADD](/wiki/Attention_deficit_disorder "Attention deficit disorder")/[ADHD](/wiki/ADHD "ADHD") and [narcolepsy](/wiki/Narcolepsy "Narcolepsy"); [dexamphetamine](/wiki/Dextroamphetamine "Dextroamphetamine") and [methylphenidate](/wiki/Methylphenidate "Methylphenidate"), are also List I drugs of the Opium Law. On the other hand, all barbiturates except for secobarbital are List II drugs, while none of them, except for phenobarbital, are prescribed today. In theory, a licensed doctor could prescribe any substance they think is needed for the correct treatment of their [patient](/wiki/Patient "Patient"), both List I and List II substances of the Opium Law, though substances which aren't available as commercial [pharmaceutical](/wiki/Pharmaceutical "Pharmaceutical") preparations have to be custom prepared by the designated [pharmacy](/wiki/Pharmacy "Pharmacy").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

All prescriptions for List I and some List II substances ([amobarbital](/wiki/Amobarbital "Amobarbital"), [buprenorphine](/wiki/Buprenorphine "Buprenorphine"), [butalbital](/wiki/Butalbital "Butalbital"), [cathine](/wiki/Cathine "Cathine"), [cyclobarbital](/wiki/Cyclobarbital "Cyclobarbital"), [flunitrazepam](/wiki/Flunitrazepam "Flunitrazepam"), [temazepam](/wiki/Temazepam "Temazepam"), [glutethimide](/wiki/Glutethimide "Glutethimide"), hemp, [pentazocine](/wiki/Pentazocine "Pentazocine") and [pentobarbital](/wiki/Pentobarbital "Pentobarbital")) of the Opium Law have to be written in full in letters, and have to contain the name and initials, address, city and telephone number of the licensed prescriber issuing the prescriptions, as well as the name and initials, address and city of the person the prescription is issued to. If the prescription is issued for an animal, the data of the owner should be used instead, and a description of the animal has to be included on the prescription.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
